coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with high quantum yield for the detection of nimesulide and fluorescence staining
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Jinping
Liang, Xiaomin
Ma, Qi
An, Jinhui
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we firstly developed a new rapid detection method of nimesulide (Nim) based on the boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) as the fluorescence probe. The B,N-CDs were prepared by a facile hydrothermal treatment using ammonium citrate and bis(pinacolato)diboron as precursors. The as-prepared B,N-CDs were mono-dispersed with an average diameter of 3.3 nm, and exhibited good stability and strong fluorescence emission with a high quantum yield of 68.89%. The fluorescence of B,N-CDs was obviously quenched with the addition of Nim. A good linear relationship between ln (F0/F) and the concentration of Nim was obtained in the range of 0–100 μM, and the limit of detection was 125 nM. Furthermore, the proposed analysis method was successfully applied for the detection of Nim in pharmaceutical samples and recoveries ranged from 94.60%–96.73%. More remarkably, it was further found that the as-prepared B,N-CDs displayed bright blue solid-state fluorescence, exhibiting potential application in invisible fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting and fluorescent dye.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B,N co-doped CDs
Nimesulide detection
High quantum yield
Invisible fluorescent ink
Fluorescent dye

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Nim in real samples
B,N-CDs for invisible fluorescent ink and fluorescent dye

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images and size distribution histogram (c) of the synthesized B,N-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of B,N-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b), N1 s (c), O1s (d) and B1s (e). (f) FT-IR spectra of B,N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of B,N-CDs. Inset is the corresponding photographs of B,N-CDs under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of B,N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) The effect of pH on the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) of B,N-CDs in the presence of 100 μM Nim. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of B,N-CDs in the absence and presence of Nim, respectively. (b) Fluorescence intensity variation of B,N-CDs with different reaction time. (c) Fluorescence spectra of B,N-CDs in the absence and presence of different concentration of Nim. (d) The plot of F0/F versus of the different concentration of Nim. (e) The linear relationship between ln (F0/F) and the concentration of Nim. (f) Fluorescence quenching effect of different substances towards B, N-CDs.

Photographs of B,N-CDs powders, paper crane staining by B,N-CDs and words written with B,N-CDs solution as fluorescent ink. a, c, e and g are taken under daylight. b, d, f and h are taken under 365 excitation.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of B,N-CDs and its potential applications.
Stern–Volmer quenching constants at different temperatures.
Determination of Nim in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescent boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with high quantum yield for the detection of nimesulide and fluorescence staining

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, Shanxi, 037009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, Shanxi, 037009, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we firstly developed a new rapid detection method of nimesulide (Nim) based on the boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) as the fluorescence probe. The B,N-CDs were prepared by a facile hydrothermal treatment using ammonium citrate and bis(pinacolato)diboron as precursors. The as-prepared B,N-CDs were mono-dispersed with an average diameter of 3.3 nm, and exhibited good stability and strong fluorescence emission with a high quantum yield of 68.89%. The fluorescence of B,N-CDs was obviously quenched with the addition of Nim. A good linear relationship between ln (F0/F) and the concentration of Nim was obtained in the range of 0–100 μM, and the limit of detection was 125 nM. Furthermore, the proposed analysis method was successfully applied for the detection of Nim in pharmaceutical samples and recoveries ranged from 94.60%–96.73%. More remarkably, it was further found that the as-prepared B,N-CDs displayed bright blue solid-state fluorescence, exhibiting potential application in invisible fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting and fluorescent dye.

B, N co-doped CDs with high quantum yield was firstly used as fluorescent probe to detect the nimesulide in the tablet. Moreover, B, N-CDs solid display strong blue fluorescence, which show potential application in invisible fluorescent ink and fluorescent dye.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, heteroatoms doping into carbon dots (CDs) has been recognized as a powerful way to modulate the properties of CDs [1,2]. Some researches have proven that doping heteroatoms (N, S, P, B, Mg, F, Si, Ln) can improve the photoluminescence (PL) performance and sensing selectivity towards targets [3–10]. For example, doping the N atom brought about the effective enhancement of quantum yield (78%) of CDs [11]. N and P atoms were co-doped into CDs via microwave heating of aniline, ethylene diamine and phosphoric acid, making CDs exhibit bright blue and green dual-emission properties [12]. Wu et al found that co-doped CDs with N and S not only improved the quantum yield of CDs, but also promoted the electron-transfer rate and coordination interaction between CDs and Fe3+ ions [13]. More recently, in order to obtain more versatile properties and applications, some researchers attempted various methods to prepare B-doped CDs. Liu's group synthesized B,N,S-co-doped CDs with red emission property by a facile one-step hydrothermal method, and successfully realized colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode detection of Fe3+ ions [14]. In Nabid's research works [15], B,N-CDs exhibited excellent catalytic activity for hydrogen generation from several common hydrogen sources. It was considered that high catalytic activity may be due to the synergistic effect of N and B dopants associated with the intrinsic properties of CDs. Even though these studies have demonstrated that B doping could improve optical or catalytical properties of CDs. However, most reported literatures showed that the B-doped CDs possessed the relative low quantum yield (QY) (see Table S1 in supporting information), which severely limited its potential applications. Thus, it is still a bottleneck to synthesize B-doped CDs with high quantum yield. Inspired by this, we attempted to prepare B,N co-doped CDs with high quantum yield by simple methods, and further explore its versatile applications.

Nimesulide (N-(4-Nitro-2-phenoxyphenyl)-methane-sulfonanilide, Nim) is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug [16,17]. It is effective in the treatment of chronic rheumatoid arthritis, inflammation of genitourinary system, otorhinolaryngological diseases, odontostomatological practice and postoperative pain [18]. Based on these properties, Nim is widely used clinically as therapeutic medicine. However, long term or excessive use of Nim will cause severe body harmful including hepatic and central nervous systems for children and adult [19]. Thus, it is quite important to detect the Nim in drugs and biological samples separation. Different analytical methods have been employed for the determination of Nim, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [20–22], electrochemical methods [23,24], spectrophotometry [25] and near infrared (NIR) spectroscopy [26]. Although these analytical techniques have been effectively used for the detection of Nim, they still face several demerits such as high cost, expensive instruments, complicated procedure and required trained personnel. Compared with the above detection methods, the fluorescence analysis method is very simple, quick, cheap, easy to handle and more sensitive. To the best of our knowledge, fluorescence determination platform of Nim based on B,N-CDs has not been reported in the literatures.

In this work, we synthesized B,N co-doped CDs by a facile hydrothermal method using ammonium citrate and bis(pinacolato)diboron as the precursors. The synthesis process is environmentally friendly, simple and efficient. Compared with the reported B-doped CDs, the as-synthesized B,N-CDs showed the relative higher fluorescence QY (68.89%) (Table S1). At the same time, the B,N-CDs was used firstly as fluorescence probe for selective and sensitive detection of Nim. In addition, in view of good stability and efficient solid-state blue emission properties, the as-synthesized CDs have been successfully utilized for fluorescent ink and dye.

Ammonium citrate (AC), Bis(pinacolato)diboron (BPDB), Nim, ibuprofen (Ibu), and 4-acetamidophenol (Ace) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). All metal salts including CdCl2, MgCl2, ZnCl2, CrCl3, AlCl3, CaCl2, BaCl2, NiCl2, PbCl2, MnCl2, HgCl2, CuCl2, FeCl2, CoCl2, FeCl3 and AgNO3 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagents Factory (Beijing, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification. Ultrapure water was used in the all experiments.

The morphology and size of B,N-CDs were observed with a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope, operating at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained with a PHI-5400 electron spectrometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (4000–500 cm−1) were recorded on a FTIR-650 spectrophotometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrometer. Fluorescence measurements were conducted with a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. Excitation and emission slit widths were both set at 5 nm.

100 mg of AC and 200 mg of BPDB were dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water, and then the solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 240 °C for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 8500 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm Millipore filter membranes to remove large particles. Subsequently, the supernatant of B,N-CDs was freeze-dried to obtain light yellow powder, and the samples was stored at 4 °C. The obtained B,N-CDs samples were dissolved in ultrapure water for further characterization and use.

The detection of Nim was carried out at room temperature in the Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH = 10). In general detection procedure, 80 μL of B,N-CDs (2 mg/mL) solution were mixed with 1920 μL of BR buffer solution containing different concentrations of Nim and the mixed solutions were incubated for 5 min at room temperature before spectral measurements. The fluorescence spectra of the system were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. In interference studies, the fluorescence spectra of B,N-CDs in presence of Ibu, Ace and different metal ions (including Cd2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Fe3+ and Ag+) were further measured by using the similar procedure.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the B,N-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of AC and BPDB at 240 °C for 10 h. The as-prepared B,N-CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (365 nm). In order to obtain B,N-CDs with the optimal optical properties, we investigated the effect of the experimental conditions including weigh ratio of raw materials, reaction temperature and reaction time. As depicted in Table S2, keeping the reaction temperature (200 °C) and reaction time (8 h) constant, it was found that the QY of the obtained B,N-CDs reached the maximum when weigh ratio of AC: BPDB is 1: 2. Under the optimal ratio of AC: BPDB, the QY of B,N-CDs increased with the increase of the reaction temperature and reaction time, and reached the maximum value at 240 °C and 10 h, respectively, then dropped gradually. The reasonable explanation is that higher reaction temperature and longer reaction time would lead to over carbonization of reaction reagents and further produce partial bulk black carbon residues, as a result, bringing about the decrease of QY value [27]. The QY of the as-synthesized B,N-CDs under optimal conditions was calculated to be approximately 68.69% by using quinine sulfate (in 0.10 M H2SO4) as a reference [28], which is higher than the QY of other reported B-doped CDs in literatures (see Table S1).

Fig. 1a depicted a TEM image of the as-obtained B,N-CDs, revealing that these CDs were quasi-spherical particles and well monodispersed in water. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1b) showed that B,N-CDs have clear lattice structures with fringe spacing of 0.26 nm, which is attributed to the (020) diffraction facet of graphitic carbon [29]. As shown in Fig. 1c, the size of the as-prepared B,N-CDs was distributed in the range of 1.5 to 6.7 nm, and the average size was ~ 3.3 nm (43.33%).

The composition of B,N-CDs was characterized using XPS technique. As shown in Fig. 2a, the XPS survey spectrum of B,N-CDs displayed four peaks at 192.23, 284.57, 400.48 and 532.07 eV, which were ascribed to B1s, C1s, N1 s, and O1s with their atomic percentages 6.07, 59.17, 5.75 and 29.01%, respectively [30]. The high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2b) of C1s indicated the presence of CB (283.8 eV), CC (284.9 eV), CN (285.8 eV), CO (286.8 eV), CO (288.0 eV) bonds on the surface of B,N-CDs [31]. As depicted in Fig. 2c, the high-resolution N1 s XPS spectrum demonstrated three peaks at 401.6, 399.8 and 398.6 eV representing graphitic N, pyrrole N and pyridine N, respectively. In the spectrum (Fig. 2d) of O1s, two peaks at 533.4 and 531.3 eV were ascribed to CO and CO. The B1s spectrum of the B,N-CDs (Fig. 2e) were deconvoluted into three peaks at 191.3, 192.14 and 193.7 eV, which were attributed to BC (sp2), BC2O and BCO2 groups, respectively [32]. The structure and composition of B,N-CDs was further characterized by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2f, two broad peaks located at 3422 and 3170 cm−1 were attributed to OH and NH stretching vibration. The peaks at 1636, 1560 cm−1 corresponded to the CO stretching vibration and NH bending vibration, respectively [33]. Moreover, the CN and BC stretching vibration was observed at 1401 and 1088 cm−1, respectively [34]. These data further confirmed that the N and B atoms have been successfully doped into CDs, and functional groups such as CN, BC, CO, CC, OH and NH coexisted on the CD's surface.

Fig. 3a depicted the UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of B,N-CDs. A slight broad absorption peak was observed at 314 nm (black line in Fig. 3a), originating from the n-π* transition of CO group, which was a typical characteristic of UV–vis absorption spectrum of fluorescent CDs. From the inset of Fig. 3a, it could be seen that the B,N-CDs aqueous solution was colorless and transparent in sunlight but exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (365 nm). In the fluorescence spectrum of B,N-CDs (blue line in Fig. 3a), the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were situated at 320 and 408 nm, respectively. In addition, it was noticed that the fluorescence peak position of B,N-CDs was unchanged while the fluorescence intensity increased initially and reached a maximum value with the increase of excitation wavelength from 300 to 320 nm. Hereafter, with further increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased accompanying with obvious red-shift of the fluorescence peak position. This excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior may be ascribed to the different particle sizes and a distribution of different surface states [35].

The fluorescence stability is great significant for the practical applications of B,N-CDs. In order to explore the stability of B,N-CDs, the influences of various factors including ionic strength, UV irradiation, pH, storage time and temperature on the fluorescence intensity of B,N-CDs were investigated detailed. As revealed in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of B,N-CDs scarcely changed along with variations of ionic strength (0–2 mol/L NaCl), UV irradiation time (0–200 min), storage time (0–18 days) and temperature (20–70 °C), demonstrated excellent storage stability and photostability. However, we found that the fluorescence intensity of B,N-CDs would gradually enhanced with increasing pH from 2 to 6, reached basically constant in range of 6–11, then dropped rapidly at pH 12 (see Fig. S1d). This significant change maybe was attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of the B,N-CDs. General speaking, such a wide pH range (6–11) can completely meet the needs in practical applications.

The high quantum yield provides a possible opportunity for B,N-CDs to serve as promising fluorescence probe. Further experimental results demonstrated that Nim could effectively quench the fluorescence of B,N-CD, implying the potential of B,N-CDs as fluorescence probe. To obtain the optimal response signal, the influence of pH value and response time on the quenching efficiency was first studied. It was seen from Fig. 4a that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) of B,N-CD in the absence and presence of Nim reached the maximum value in pH 10 BR buffer solution. Therefore, pH 10 BR buffer solution was used in the followed research work. Fig. 4b displayed that the fluorescence of B,N-CDs was quenched rapidly by Nim within 2 min. In subsequent experiments, 5 min was selected as the optimum reaction time to obtain stable and accurate experimental results.

We further evaluated the sensitivity and selectivity of the fluorescence sensor towards the detection of Nim. As shown in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence intensity of B,N-CDs at 408 nm decreased gradually with increasing the concentration of Nim. Fig. 4d presented the relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity ratio and Nim concentration. A good linear response between ln (F0/F) and the concentration of Nim were observed in the range from 0 to100 μM (Fig. 4e). The linear regression equation was expressed as ln (F0/F) = 0.01801CNim (μM) + 0.00444 (R = 0.9999). The detection limit was calculated to be 1.25 × 10−7 M at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3σ. In addition, the fluorescence quenching ability of various interference substances (including Cd2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Ag+ ions, Ibu and Ace) were further investigated under the same experimental conditions. It clearly demonstrated that the addition of Nim could cause the significant fluorescence quenching effect, while fluorescence quenching ability of other interference substances was negligible (Fig. 4f). These results suggested that the developed sensor has excellent selectivity and high sensitivity.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism was usually classified as dynamic quenching originated from collisional encounters between the fluorophore and quencher, and static quenching resulted from the formation of a nonfluorescent fluorophore-quencher complex in the ground state. Generally, the dynamic quenching constants will increase with the rise of the system temperature owing to collision between fluorescent substance and quencher [36]. By contrast, the static quenching constants will decrease with the rise of temperature. The corresponding fluorescence quenching constant can be calculated by the Stern–Volmer equation [37]:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of B,N-CDs in the absence and presence of Nim. K
SV and K
q are the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and the bimolecular reaction rate constant, respectively. [Q] is the concentration of Nim in the reaction system. τ 0 is the lifetime of the fluorophore without any other the quenchers, which has a general value of 10−8 s. Fig. S2 showed Stern–Volmer plots between F0/F and the concentration of Nim at different temperature. Table 1 displayed the corresponding K
SV and K
q value at different temperature. It was noticed that Stern–Volmer quenching constants increased with the rise of the system temperature, revealing that the fluorescence quenching process was a dynamic quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Nim in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the practicality of the developed fluorescence sensor, standard recovery experiments were performed to test the content of Nim in a pharmaceutical sample. In typically, Nim tablets were grinded uniformly in mortar, 125 mg of the powder was dissolved into 1 mL of 1 mol/L NaOH, and further diluted to 25 mL with pH 10 BR buffer solution. The detection of Nim was carried out according to the 2.4 section. For recovery experiments, the standard solution of Nim was added into the above resulting solution and detected according to the previously proposed method [38]. All of the recoveries were calculated according to the equation below:
Recovery
%
=
C measured
−
C intial
/
C added
× 100 %
The recovery rates of Nim are in the range of 94.60%–96.73% (Table 2 ), implying fluorescence method has potential applicability for the determination of Nim in pharmaceutical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: B,N-CDs for invisible fluorescent ink and fluorescent dye
ce:section.ce:para: We further explored the potential application of B,N-CDs in invisible fluorescent ink and fluorescent dye. As depicted in Fig. 5 , it was obviously seen that B,N-CDs was light yellow powder under daylight, but it exhibited bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation. The paper crane made of filter paper staining by B,N-CDs also presented dramatic blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation. Furthermore, words handwritten on ordinary filter paper using B,N-CDs solution as fluorescent ink were invisible under daylight but displayed visible blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation. And these fluorescent words could keep for five months without any fade, this stable “vis-invisible” and “UV–visible” properties of B,N-CDs benefit their long-term applications in the fluorescent staining and the anticounterfeit field [39–42].

In summary, B,N-CDs with strong blue fluorescence and high quantum yield was synthesized by a facile hydrothermal approach. The obtained B,N-CDs presented excellent photostability and storage stability. Based on the dramatic fluorescence quenching capacity of Nim, a sensitive and selective fluorescence sensing platform for Nim was developed. The linear detection range is 0–100 μM, and the detection limit is 125 nM. The proposed analysis method was successfully applied for the determination of Nim in pharmaceutical samples. Moreover, owing to the excellent solid-state fluorescence property, B,N-CDs can be used as fluorescent ink and fluorescent dyes, which may expand its potential applications in the fluorescent staining and the anticounterfeit fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21301111), the Natural Science Foundation of Datong City of China (2017129) and research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2016-105).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.03.074.
